rulings.Case Title: BRIAN MOLONEY VS. RICHARD SCHUMACHER et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT /Adjudication And Evidence
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Wednesday, December 4, 2013, Line 13: DEFENDANT STATE FARM GENERAL INSURANCE COMPANY, BUSINESS ENTITY UNKNOWN'S MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT / Adjudication And Evidence Motion for summary judgment granted. As to the breach of contract cause of action, Defendant has met its burden by showing that Plaintiff cannot establish the existence of a contract between the parties. (Undisputed Facts nos. 8-10; Aguilar v. Atlantic Richfield Co. (2001) 25 Cal.4th 826, 853.) This shifted the burden to Plaintiff to show a triable issue of material fact. (Id.) Plaintiff has not met its burden, as no evidence was provided showing that Plaintiff was a party to the contract or an intended third-party beneficiary. Plaintiff's claim that he is an intended beneficiary because he is the tenant of the insured is insufficient. The terms of the policy must "contemplate a benefit to the third person." (Spinks v. Equity Residential Briarwood Apartments (2009) 171 Cal.App.4th 1004, 1022; see also Jones v. Aetna Casualty & Surety Co. (1994) 26 Cal.App.4th 1717.) The breach of the covenant of good faith and fair dealing cause of action also fails because Plaintiff has not shown he is a party to the contract or an intended third-party beneficiary. (Infinet Marketing Services, Inc. v. American Motorist Ins. Co. (2007) 150 Cal.App.4th 168, 180, "¿an insurer's duty of good faith and fair dealing is owed solely to its insured and, perhaps, any express beneficiary of the insurance policy.") Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order with the name and address of the prevailing party's counsel or the prevailing party if pro per in the top left of the first page of proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and in compliance with Code of Civil Procedure 437c(g) and must bring the proposed order to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =302/MJM

rulings.Case Title: RIVERWALK HOLDINGS, LTD VS. AREZOU SHADABADI AKA AREZOU ABDOLAZIM et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Ntc Of Hearing On Claim Of Exemption; Ntc Of Oppos.
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Wednesday, December 4, 2013, Line 15: PLAINTIFF RIVERWALK HOLDINGS, LTD'S Hearing On Claim Of Exemption; Notice of Opposition Denied. Ms. Shadabadi has not demonstrated that she is entitled to a claim of exemption. (CCP § 703.080(b).) Defendant has not shown why the $101.50 currently held by the sheriff's office is used in her trade, business or profession. (Claim of Exemption 4.) The Sheriff shall release the funds currently held to Plaintiff. rulings.Case Title: ROGER LOUIS CRAWFORD VS. SAN FRANCISCO HOUSING AUTHORITY et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Defendant Henry Alvarez, Iii'S Motion To Change Venue
rulings.Rulings: On calendar for Wednesday, December 4, 2013, Line 28, Defendant Henry Alvarez, III'S Motion To Change Venue Defendant's motion to transfer venue is denied. In his memorandum of points and authorities, Defendant moves for change of venue pursuant to CCP §392. It is apparent that Defendant means CCP §397(b) ("When there is reason to believe that an impartial trial cannot be had therein.") Defendant has not met his burden of showing that a change of venue is warranted. (See Nguyen v. Superior Court (1996) 49 Cal. App. 4th 1781, 1791.) The anecdotal evidence in the form of a limited number of news articles -- the most recent of which is from May 2013 - are insufficient to show that defendant cannot receive an impartial jury trial. Defendant's characterization of a handful of articles as "viciously negative" and an "editorial pogrom" is not sufficient to demonstrate a "widespread feeling of prejudice extending over a long time." (Nguyen, supra, at 1791.) The statistical evidence, in the form of an on-line survey, is also insufficient. The data is not a random sample, it asked leading questions, and there is no way to determine whether the respondents were a representative sample of San Francisco jurors. Further, the on-line survey failed to ask whether the respondents could be fair and impartial jurors. The Court credits the declaration of Alex Evans in opposition to the motion to transfer, which sets forth the methodological flaws in defendant's survey evidence. Further, even if the evidence in the on-line survey were meaningful (and the Court finds on balance that it is not), the data is subject to different interpretations and conclusions. Defendant's polling expert says "40% of those polled had seen or heard some media coverage of the SFHA prior to the poll." (Bradley Declaration in support of Motion, 7.) That means that 60% had not. Finally, the experience in the recent Myers trial, as anecdotally relayed by Defendant, does not suggest that Defendant could not have an impartial trial in this County. It is a commonplace to instruct jurors not to read any media coverage, whether in print or on-line, and to take steps to ensure that this order is complied with during trial if circumstances suggest that the order might have been violated. Overall, considering all of the evidence, Defendant has not shown that he could not have an impartial trial in San Francisco in 2014. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4 p.m. stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order with the name and address of the prevailing party's counsel or the prevailing party if pro per in the top left of the first page of proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =302/MJM

rulings.Case Title: TIM LARSEN VS. SAN FRANCISCO HOUSING AUTHORITY et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Defendant Henry Alvarez, Iii'S Motion To Change Venue
rulings.Rulings: On calendar for Wednesday, December 4, 2013, Line 29, Defendant Henry Alvarez, Iii'S Motion To Change Venue Defendant's motion to transfer venue is denied. In his memorandum of points and authorities, Defendant moves for change of venue pursuant to CCP §392. It is apparent that Defendant means CCP §397(b) ("When there is reason to believe that an impartial trial cannot be had therein.") Defendant has not met his burden of showing that a change of venue is warranted. (See Nguyen v. Superior Court (1996) 49 Cal. App. 4th 1781, 1791.) The anecdotal evidence in the form of a limited number of news articles -- the most recent of which is from May 2013 - are insufficient to show that defendant cannot receive an impartial jury trial. Defendant's characterization of a handful of articles as "viciously negative" and an "editorial pogrom" is not sufficient to demonstrate a "widespread feeling of prejudice extending over a long time." (Nguyen, supra, at 1791.) The statistical evidence, in the form of an on-line survey, is also insufficient. The data is not a random sample, it asked leading questions, and there is no way to determine whether the respondents were a representative sample of San Francisco jurors. Further, the on-line survey failed to ask whether the respondents could be fair and impartial jurors. The Court credits the declaration of Ben Tulchin in opposition to the motion to transfer, which sets forth the methodological flaws in defendant's survey evidence. Further, even if the evidence in the on-line survey were meaningful (and the Court finds on balance that it is not), the data is subject to different interpretations and conclusions. Defendant's polling expert says "40% of those polled had seen or heard some media coverage of the SFHA prior to the poll." (Bradley Declaration in support of Motion, 7.) That means that 60% had not. Finally, the experience in the recent Myers trial, as anecdotally relayed by Defendant, does not suggest that Defendant could not have an impartial trial in this County. It is a commonplace to instruct jurors not to read any media coverage, whether in print or on-line, and to take steps to ensure that this order is complied with during trial if circumstances suggest that the order might have been violated. Overall, considering all of the evidence, Defendant has not shown that he could not have an impartial trial in San Francisco in 2014. rulings.Case Title: FARMERS INSURANCE EXCHANGE, VS. RAYMOND TANG et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Mtn To Compel Answers To Foirm Interoggs
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Wednesday, December 4, 2013, Line 1 - PLAINTIFF FARMERS INSURANCE EXCHANGE's Motion To Compel Answers To Form Interrogatories. Pro Tem Judge Nils Rosenquest, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Granted. No opposition filed. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to nrosenquest@earthlink.net with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JPT)

If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Granted. No opposition filed. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to nrosenquest@earthlink.net with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: MICHAEL KULICK MD VS. SAINT FRANCIS MEMORIAL HOSPITAL et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Further Discovery Responses And Document Production And For Sanctions
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Wednesday, December 4, 2013, Line 3 - PLAINTIFF MICHAEL KULICK's Motion To Compel Further Discovery Responses And Document Production And For Sanctions. Pro Tem Judge Nils Rosenquest, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Hearing required. Court requests that the parties: (1) meet and confer and provide the Court a list of any agreed production or further interrogatory responses, (2) be prepared to address the criteria for ESI specified in CCP Sec. 2031.310, particularly with respect to good cause, the manner in which the ESI has been stored, and the criteria listed in subsection (g), and (3) the time and expense necessary to produce the ESI data in usable form, (4) privacy concerns affecting patient records (balancing plaintiff's cause for production of records against patients' interests). For the convenience of the Court, the parties are also requested to provide copies of any federal authorities cited in the motion papers per CRC 3.1112(i). =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: GUSTAVO RIOS VS. CERCLE DE L'UNION, A CALIFORNIA CORPORATION DOING et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: DEMURRER TO COMPLAINT
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Wednesday, December 4, 2013, Line 19: DEFENDANT CERCLE DE L'UNION, DBA THE FRENCH CLUB'S (THE FRENCH CLUB) DEMURRER TO COMPLAINT The demurrers to the 7th cause of action for intentional infliction of emotional distress, 8th cause of action for negligent infliction of emotional distress, and 10th cause of action for wrongful termination are sustained without leave to amend. Plaintiff concedes these causes of action are time barred pursuant to CCP 335.1. The demurrer to the 9th cause of action for violation of FEHA is overruled. Defendant fails to show that its settlement with the DFEH bars plaintiff's claim. The letter of June 6, 2012, states that the right to sue notice remained unchanged. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. rulings.Case Title: FROILAN FRIAS et al VS. PROVEN MANAGEMENT INC. et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion For Change Of Venue
rulings.Rulings: On calendar for Wednesday, December 4, 2013, Line 24, DEFENDANT PROVEN MANAGEMENT INC., JORDAN ERICKSON'S Motion For Change Of Venue Off calendar as untimely under CCP § 1005(b). The motion was filed and served by fax and overnight delivery on November 12, 2013. Defendants provided only 14 court days notice. To the extent that Defendants assert that Plaintiff waived notice, Defendant failed to seek relief from the Court to hear this matter on shortened time. Defendant may file a new motion. rulings.Case Title: JUNYING YU VS. WILLIAM BAILES et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Ntc Of Mtn And Mtn To Reconsider The Court'S Order Of Nov 19, 2013, Based Upon The Order Of This Court'S Appellate Division, And To Enter A New And Different Order, And For Other Relief
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Wednesday, December 4, 2013, Line 27: DEFENDANTS WILLIAM BAILES' and GIGI ELLIS' Motion To Reconsider The Court's Order Of November 19, 2013, Based Upon The Order Of This Court's Appellate Division, And To Enter A New And Different Order, And For Other Relief Defendants' motion for reconsideration is granted in part. Pursuant to the order of the Appellate Division dated November 19, 2013, this Court's Order Vacating Default and Default Judgment and Quashing and Recalling Writ of Execution for Possession of 433 Nevada Street, San Francisco CA, filed November 19, 2013 has been vacated to the extent that it required Defendants to file and serve their answer by 2:00 p.m. on November 20, 2013. Consistent with the Appellate Division order, the Court reconsiders its November 19, 2013 order and strikes the Answer filed by Defendants on November 19, 2013. Defendants filed their Answer on November 19, 2013 after entry of this Court's Order of November 19, 2013 but before receiving the Appellate Division's order of November 19, 2013. The remaining requests for reconsideration of the Court's November 19, 2013 order is denied. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4 p.m. stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order with the name and address of the prevailing party's counsel or the prevailing party if pro per in the top left of the first page of proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =302/MJM
